text:
The present theoretical-empirical study delves into the phenomenon of individual humanitarian giving, focusing on the example of the Long-Distance Adoption project realized in Karnataka, India, and managed by the Diocesan Catholic Charity in Hradec Králové, Czech Republic. The study employs a transcultural approach drawing on the resources of cultural and philosophical anthropology and personalism. This allows for the exploration of the project from two planes: the horizontal plane which respects the diversity of cultures, and the vertical plane which enters into the metaphysical dimension of human dignity and dialogue, which is independent of cultural context.

The study uses the social construction of reality as a theoretical basis and is based on qualitative research methods – specifically, on the basis of interviews with donors and participant observation. It specifically deals with the construction of a surplus child through the donors’ speeches and the social rules behind the choice of a child in the virtual adoption portals, as well as the donor-recipient relation. A large part of the analysis deals with correspondence, which the authors describe as a paradoxical connection where donors express their desire for a human and authentic connection with the recipient family and the child, which is however consistently disrupted by the anonymous nature of the exchange, as well as by existing power relations and social stereotypes embedded in the adoption process.

This paper concludes by highlighting several factors which might support or endanger transcultural aims, showing a constant interplay between universalistic humanitarian intentions (e.g. aiming for gender equality or support to the right to education) and particular sociocultural contexts in the case of TC donation projects, highlighting at the end the ambivalence of these project as an approach to establishing readiness toward the "otherness" of distant human beings, enabling them - through an always on-going and ongoing critical observation and revision in respect to preventing any form of paternalism and stereotypes of reductionism potentially forming in the wake of this very generous opportunity for dialogue between different peoples - to perform a dialogic work on differences as the real chance guaranteeing the success of the intercultural organisation and practice so dear to so many authors on the world wide web.
